coredata.dc:title: An optical nose based on array of metal-doped carbon dots for identification of hazardous amines and assessing meat freshness
coredata.dc:creator: Alimohammadi, Motahareh
Sharifi, Hoda
Tashkhourian, Javad
Vazan, Mohammad
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Hemmateenejad, Bahram
coredata.dc:description: There is an obvious pressing demand for the sensitive and rapid, identification of toxic gases as environmental concerns. In this study, a facile approach for the detection and identification of amine solvents is reported. A colorimetric sensor array composed of different ninhydrin-mediated transition metal-doped carbon dots (TMCDs), deposited on a paper substrate, could sniff the odor of 29 different amines and accomplish their accurate identification. This analytical device generates a unique response pattern for each amine. Modeling the response data of the sensor array by linear discriminate analysis yields a discrimination accuracy of 94 % for the amines. Also, this method allows rapid quantification of ethylamine and isopropylamine in the concentration ranges of 0.008–1600 ppm and 0.05–828 ppm with detection limits of 1.4 ppb and 39 ppb, respectively, which are well below the immediately dangerous to life or health (IDLH) limit concentration for these amines. Interestingly, this sensor array provides discrimination between the isomers of some amines. Moreover, binary and ternary mixtures of amine solvents could be well recognized with this sensor. The application of the sensor array was tested for the assessment of the quality and freshness of some kinds of meat.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Volatile organic compounds (VOC)
Sensor array
Optical nose
Metal doped carbon dots
Ninhydrin
Chemometric methods

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of studied transition metal-doped carbon dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thankfully acknowledge financial support from Shiraz University Research Council .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of whole processes of illustration of the sensor array for amines detection. (b) The place of the sensing units on the µ-PAD. (c) Actual dimensions of used PAD.
(a)UV absorption spectrum of Transition metal–doped CD, (b) TEM image of carbon dots, (c) the FTIR spectra of Transition metal–doped CD, and (d) XRD of Transition metal–doped CD.
(a) Images of PAD before and after exposure to 29 amines and (b) corresponding colorimetric difference maps.
(a) PCA, (b) HCA, and (c) LDA analysis for twenty-nine amines.
LDA analysis for twenty-nine amines as primary, secondary, tertiary, and ammonia.
PCA plots for the (a) primary, (b) secondary, and (c) tertiary amines with accuracy of 100 %.

(a) PCA and (b) HCA plot for the ternary, binary mixtures of three different amines (nBA, DMA, and TEA) with an accuracy of 100 %. (c) Corresponding color change patterns of the sensor array for these mixtures.

Color change patterns of the sensor array for (a) EA and (b) iPA at different concentrations and Euclidean distances of the sensor array plotted against the different concentrations of these two amines (with logarithmic relationship).
PCA score plots and images of sensor array after exposure to (a) meat of fish, (b) lamb, and (c) chicken stored at (i) room & (ii) refrigerator temperature.
Comparison with other reported sensors for amine detection.

head.ce:title: An optical nose based on array of metal-doped carbon dots for identification of hazardous amines and assessing meat freshness

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, the Islamic Republic of Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, 71454, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University Kermanshah, the Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Razi University Kermanshah

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Razi University Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an obvious pressing demand for the sensitive and rapid, identification of toxic gases as environmental concerns. In this study, a facile approach for the detection and identification of amine solvents is reported. A colorimetric sensor array composed of different ninhydrin-mediated transition metal-doped carbon dots (TMCDs), deposited on a paper substrate, could sniff the odor of 29 different amines and accomplish their accurate identification. This analytical device generates a unique response pattern for each amine. Modeling the response data of the sensor array by linear discriminate analysis yields a discrimination accuracy of 94 % for the amines. Also, this method allows rapid quantification of ethylamine and isopropylamine in the concentration ranges of 0.008–1600 ppm and 0.05–828 ppm with detection limits of 1.4 ppb and 39 ppb, respectively, which are well below the immediately dangerous to life or health (IDLH) limit concentration for these amines. Interestingly, this sensor array provides discrimination between the isomers of some amines. Moreover, binary and ternary mixtures of amine solvents could be well recognized with this sensor. The application of the sensor array was tested for the assessment of the quality and freshness of some kinds of meat.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Amines are widely used in the pharmaceutical industry, dyeing, military operations, and materials chemistry. An excess amount of them is toxic and hazardous to human health and the environment [1]. The National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) has reported the maximum recommended exposure for amines tolerance as 10 ppm for long-term exposure of 10 h and 15 ppm for short-term exposure of 15 min [2]. Over the past decades, detecting organic amines at the trace vapor level has been crucial for the quality control of food [3], air pollution monitoring [4], and even medical diagnosis [5]. That's why there is an urgent demand for real-time and on-spot monitoring of these compounds.

Previous works in the detection of amines, including electrochemical devices [6], and gas or liquid chromatography [7,8], usually suffer from some limitations due to complicated instrumentation, time-consuming operations, and clumsy pre-treatment of samples. Among these various detection technologies, the use of colorimetric sensors [9] represents a cost-effective, simple, and highly sensitive detection strategy. While sensitive detection of individual amine compounds has been previously reported [10], selective discrimination within a larger family of amines is not easily achieved. Previous methods to selectively discriminate amines, including sensors based on functionalized mesoporous silica [11], molecular imprinting [10,12], and chromogenic reagents [13], have been reported.

One of the alternative approaches for effective sensing of amine solvents is using electronic noses by mimicking the mammalian olfactory and gustatory systems. As opposed to ‘lock-key’ specific recognition, this strategy relies on cross-responsive sensors. In other words, it creates various signals and provides a unique pattern for the identification of individual analytes.

Paper-based analytical devices (PADs) have attracted myriad attention due to their ease of operation, low cost, portability, powerful discrimination, and rapid response. It combines the advantages of PADs and colorimetric sensor array, and extends its application fields in analytical chemistry [14]. The pioneering work on identification and distinguishing different classes of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) back to 2000′s, when Suslick and co-workers reported a facile method based on optical probes [15]. The basic system of paper-based analytical devices consists of a variety of colorimetric indicators (e.g., solvatochromic dyes, pH indicators, metalloporphyrin derivatives, etc.), based on property changes (e.g., mass, volume, and conductivity). This technology can be applied in situations where it is not necessary to know a VOCs’s composition in detail but is instead used to detect changes in VOCs characteristics, even in a complex (sometimes interfering) background. Many colorimetric or fluorometric sensing array for discriminating biogenic amines have been reported to investigate food spoilage [16–19].

Carbon dots are among the innovative nanomaterials that have been proposed as sensing elements for fabricating various sensor array. Because of outstanding and novel properties such as facile synthesis and fascinating photoluminescence, remarkable biocompatibility, and excellent photostability [20], these compounds, have gained widespread attention since their discovery eighteen years ago [21], Transition metal-doped carbon dots with an inherent photoluminescence property relative to carbon dots have received considerable attention [22]. The importance of metal ion-containing sensor compounds in an array is confirmed by the recent indications that the mammalian olfactory receptors are in fact metalloproteins [23] and the most odiferous toxic compounds often bind readily to metal ions [15].

Very recently, we have developed different kind of optical sensor arrays based on nanomaterials for food, environment and health monitoring [24–27]. In continuation of our work on development of novel nanomaterial-based optical sensor arrays, in the current study, ninhydrin-mediated transition metal-doped carbon dots (TMCDs) have been used as novel sensing platform for fabrication of optical sensor arrays. The developed sensor array was applied for the detection and identification of twenty-nine amines comprising aliphatic branched, cyclic, and aromatic structures. The TMCD-based sensor array combines the sensitivity of state-of-the-art detectors with the ability to distinguish various amines, even some isomers, with the naked eye for visual inspection. To analyze the cumulative array responses, chemometric methods including principal component analysis (PCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) were employed. Upon exposure to the amine vapors, the sensor array showed remarkable color changes that were specific to different types of amines. This gas sensor was tested for quantitative detection by determining the concentration of isopropyl amine (iPA) and ethyl amine (EA). Finally, we checked the capability of our sensor array to evaluate meat freshness. According to the best of our knowledge, this sensor array is the first report of a colorimetric sensor array with the ability to classify amines based on their types (primary, secondary, and tertiary).
Some general parts relating to materials and equipment are given in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of studied transition metal-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Transition metal-doped carbon dots (TMCDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. In brief, a mixture of 10.0 mg of bovine serum albumin (BSA) dissolved in 5.0 mL of deionized water, 10.0 mg of a typical transition metal salt, and 5.0 mL of ethanol were transferred to a reaction vessel. This mixture was sonicated to achieve a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution was placed into a 30-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven to react at 180 °C for 12 h. An almost brown solution was collected after the mixture was cooled to room temperature and centrifugated at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was the TMCD.

The working principle of our system is shown in Fig. 1a. The sensor array device is composed of the solutions of nine sensing elements (1:2 vol mixtures of TMCDs to Nin) deposited on the petals of the flower-like PAD. The pattern of the PAD was created by AutoCAD software and then printed on Whatman filter paper. The printed patterns were then cut using a laser cutter. Dimensions and the actual image of the PAD can be seen in Fig. 1a. A 0.35 µL aliquot solution of every sensing element was transferred to the corresponding pedal of the flower-like PAD. A paper stand was used to keep the PAD steady in a sample tube like a fritillary flower.

As depicted in Fig. 1a, the instructions for the method are very simple. After depositing the sensing units on the PAD’s petal, the device’s image was recorded (before image). Then, 300 µL of the amino solvent of a specified concentration was introduced to a 2-mL microcentrifuge tube, and the cap with the installed sensor array device was tightened. The color change of sensing units after exposure to the vapor of amines was captured after one hour (after image).

Fresh meats of fish, lamb, and chicken breast fillet were prepared from local grocery stores in Shiraz, Iran. For this study, a 0.5 g portion of each meat sample was placed in a sealed 2-mL microcentrifuge tube, with an installed sensor array on the cap and let it for 1 h to accumulate volatiles prior to measurements. According to [28], each meat sample was stored either at room temperature (24 ± 1 °C) or in the kitchen refrigerator (4 ± 1 °C) for a length of time varying from 0 to 11 days. Three independent trials were run for each meat sample.

The average color intensities in the red, green, and blue (RGB) format over all pixels of the sensing zones were extracted using ImageJ software. The difference between images ‘before’ and ‘after’ exposure to the vapor of amines/meat samples was then calculated. So, considering 9 sensing elements, each represented by 3 color values, the response of every sensor array device was represented by a row vector of 27 elements. Herein, we investigated 29 amine molecules, each in three replicate assays. Therefore, for the entire data set, a data matrix size of 87 × 27 was provided. To evaluate the ability of the sensor array in the recognition and monitoring of amines, the data matrix was subjected to different multivariate pattern recognition methods, including principal component analysis (PCA), hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), and linear discriminant analysis (LDA). PCA and HCA are both unsupervised exploratory data analyses, i.e., “clustering”: PCA attempts to project data into as few dimensions as possible, while HCA is commonly used to evaluate the “dissimilarity” among data points and cluster them in multivariate vector space. LDA as a supervised data analysis was also used to classify the amines based on pre-defined class membership [29,30].

The UV-visible absorption spectra of transition metal-doped carbon dots and incorporated carbon dots (CDs) are shown in Fig. 2a. The localized resonance absorption of carbon dots and transition metal-doped carbon dots exhibits a broad characteristic peak observed at 345 nm due to n-π * transition originating from the CO groups and also due to the contribution of other surface-enveloped functional groups. A dip was also observed at 280 nm due to π-π * electronic transition due to the surface bending characteristic of graphitic sp2-carbon on C-dot surfaces. It can be noticed that the absorption maximum of the carbon dots doped with metal atoms showed a redshift in the absorption spectrum.

The size and morphology of the carbon dots were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as presented in Fig. 2b. The TEM images revealed that the majority of particles exhibited nearly spherical morphologies with a size range of less than 20 nm. X-ray diffraction peaks of pristine C-dots and the transition metal-doped (Fig. 2c) represented a broad amorphous (002) peak at 26.55° for pristine C-dots, which agreed with the previous reports [31,32]. The peaks of the synthesized Cr3+/C, Mn2+/C, Ni2+/C, Cu2+/C-dots exhibited a minor increase to 29.55°, 31.85°, 29.3°, and 28.55°, and those of Fe3+/C, Co2+/C and Zn2+/C-dots showed a little decrease to 25.90°, 24.80°, and 29.15°, respectively. In all metal-doped carbon dots, a shoulder was appeared in the peaks that there was not presented in the pristine CD. The existence of main functional groups present in different sets of transition metal-doped carbon dots was confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2d). The presence of carboxylic groups can be demonstrated by the broad peak centered around 3380 cm −1 which is the vibrations of the −OH group and the stretching vibrations of CO around 1550 cm−1.

The PL emission spectra of pristine C-dots and TMCDs (Cr3+/C, Mn2+/C, Fe3+/C, Co2+/C, Ni2+ /C, Cu 2+ /C, and Zn2+ /C dots) are shown in Fig. S1 (a-g). The photoluminescence properties of CDs depend on the HOMO-LUMO energy band gap and could be enhanced by introducing metal ions. Since metals have a single electron with a large atomic radius, they become electron donors. So, upon metal ion doping into CDs and charge transporting between metal ions and the graphene matrix of carbon dot, the physicochemical properties of CDs, as well as photoluminescence properties, are changed [33].

As one can see from Fig. S1 (a-g), the PL emission of CDs in the presence of transition metals was quenched, while the PL of CDs doped with transition metals was almost enhanced. This confirmed that the coordination of metals doped in CDs differed from those added to the solution of CDs. Like most luminescent CDs, the TMCDs had an excitation-dependent PL with emission ranging over 413–470 nm for 340–400 nm excitation (Fig. S2 (a-g)) because of surface states that affect the band gap.

The occurrence of transition metal ions in TMCDs was further confirmed by EDX analysis (Fig. S3). The obtained weight percentages of nitrogen and oxygen for the synthesized TMCDs were attributed to amine and oxy functional groups formed on the CDs surfaces and were consistent with the shown FTIR results. The incorporation of transition metal ions onto CDs was notably validated with weight percentages of 2.69 % of Cr3+ in Cr3+/C-dots, 8.51 % of Mn2+ in Mn2+/C-dots, 2.83 % of Fe3+ in Fe3+/C-dots, 10.81 % of Co2+ in Co2+/C-dots, 0.81 % of Ni2+ in Ni2+/C-dots, 12.06 % of Cu2+ in Cu2+/C-dots, and 7.44 % of Zn2+ in Zn2+/C-dots.

Since TMCDs do not adsorb light in the visible region, they are not colorful and hence cannot be used to fabricate colorimetric sensors. Therefore, we used ninhydrin (Nin) as a mediator to make colorful sensor arrays. I addition, Nin can add extra selectivity to the system as explained in the following. Nin, as a strong electrophile reagent, reacts with nucleophiles such as amines, amides, and amino acids to produce a highly colored, condensation product known as Ruhemann's purple [34]. In our sensing system, Nin, on the one hand, can interact with the amine functional groups on the surface of the TMCDs and in the other hand with the amines as analytes in the vapor phase. So, the addition of Nin to TMCDs provides three competitive reactions: the charge transfer reaction between amines and Nin, the coordination of amines with TMCDs, and the charge transfer reaction between Nin and TMCDs.

The image of the sensing elements at the end of the sensing device’s petals (Fig. 1b) clearly demonstrates the differential interaction of TMCDs with Nin. Both Nin and TMCDs solutions are colorless in the solution phase or when deposited on paper. However, mixing TMCDs with Nin generates colorful products. The formation of a light purple color between Nin and the CDs without metal doping is most probably due to a charge transfer reaction between Nin and different amino functional groups at the surface of the CDs, coming from the decomposition of BSA. It is observed from Fig. 1b that the doped metals had a significant effect on the mentioned interactions. Among the used TMCDs, those doped by Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, and Zn2+ showed more distinct color changes with respect to the non-doped CDs. These competitive reactions, which differ among amines, add extra cross-selectivity from a chemistry point of view.

The response of the optical sensor arrays depends on reaction time and reaction temperature. When the sensor is exposed to the vapor of the amines, sometime is required for saturation of the solution headspace from the analyte’s vapor. Also, diffusion of the analyte’s vapor into the sensors’ texture and reaction with the sensing elements need some times. On the other hand, the analyte’s volatility and also analyte-sensor reaction equilibria can be affected by temperature. Therefore, every method based on optical sensor arrays requires finding optimal values for these experimental conditions. To optimize the time, TBA, as an amine with a high boiling point (214 ºC), was chosen. Fig. S4a shows the response of the array to 300 µL of TBA gas as a function of time, indicating that one hour was required for this analyte to reach the maximum response. Although higher temperatures can decrease the measuring time (here, less than 1 h), we preferred to work at room temperature due to the flammability and explosive potential of amines.

Besides reaction time and temperature, the amount of ninhydrin added to the solutions of TMCDs was also optimized. To do so, several solutions of different volume ratios of Nin to TMCDs were prepared, and their effect on the response of the sensor array for discrimination of the amines was investigated. The accuracy of the PCA-LDA model was used to find the best conditions. According to the data shown in Fig. S4b, the volume ratio of 2:1 (Nin to TMCDs), which represented the highest accuracy for discrimination of the amines, was selected for the next analyses. To understand the importance of ninhydrin in discrimination, all sensors were injected onto the paper in the absence of Nin, and the pad was exposed to a number of amines. The results in Fig. S5 showed that all sensors except Mn2+/CDs did not show a colorimetric response, emphasizing the central role of Nin in functioning the sensor array.

An artificial nose based on TMCDs was designed for the detection and identification of a large number of amines. The used TMCDs represented differential interactions with amines and could be considered as cross-reactive reagents for the design of sensor array. Amines are Brønsted and Lewis bases, but their base strength can be significantly modified by substituents. The quantitative evaluation of basicity often involves comparing the pKa values of conjugate acids, where a higher pKa signifies a stronger base. Due to the presence of metal centers, the TMCDs possess the capability to accept electrons and function as acids in this reaction. Consequently, the TMCDs interact with various amines having distinct pKa values and varying base strengths through acid-base interaction. The real image of the sensor array PAD is shown in Fig. 1b. As seen, the device is composed of the mixtures of seven metal-doped carbon dots with Nin as the main sensing element. Moreover, the mixture of non-doped CDs with Nin was used as another sensing element. To include the interaction of the analytes with ninhydrin alone, on one petal of the PAD, Nin solution in the absence of any CDs was deposited. So, in total, nine sensing elements were used.

The structure of the 29 studied amines and their pKa are shown in Fig. S6. Primary amines like methylamine (MA), ethylamine (EA) n-butylamine (nBA), benzylamine (BZA), sec-butyl amine (sBA), cyclohexylamine (CHA), isopropylamine (iPA), tertbutylamine (tBA), ethylenediamine (EDA), triethylenetetramine (TETA), aniline (AN), 2-chloro aniline (2-CAN), secondary amines like dimethylamine (DMA), diethylamine (DEA), diethanolamine (DEOA), diisopropylamine (DiPA), pyrolidine (PYL), pipyridine (PPY) and n, n-dicyclohexylamine (n,n-DCHA), tertiary amines like trimethylamine (TMA), tributylamine (TBA), triethylamine (TEA), n,n,n,n-tetramethylethylenediamine (n,n,n,n-TMEDA), n,n-dimethylcyclohexylamine (n,n-DMCHA), n-benzyldimethylamine ( n-BZDMA), p
-dimethylaminobenzaldehyde (pDMABZ), pyridine (PY) and 3,4-dimethyl pyridine (3,4-DMPY). The Brønsted and Lewis acid/base reaction between the TMCD and amines forms a different compound with distinct colors and obtains distinct visual patterns for different amines.

Fig. 3a represents changing the color of sensing units after interacting with these 29 studied amines. Interestingly, the sensing elements on the PADs showed obvious color changes when interacting with the vapor of the amines. Each analyte exhibited distinctive color changes, allowing for straightforward differentiation of the studied amines through visual inspection. For example, all sensor elements 1–9 were changed to brown for ammonia, MA, EA, iPA, nBA, sBA, EDA, BZA, DMA, DEA, DiPA, PY, CHA, and PPY, but with different color intensities. On the other hand, they were changed to purple for AN, 2-CAN, TEA, TBA, n,n-DMCHA, 3,4-DMPY,n-BZDMA, n,n-DCHA, n,n,n,n-TMEDA, tBA, TETA, DEOA, yellow for TMA, PYL, and pink for p-DMABZ. Impressively, nBA and sBA, which are structural isomers, behaved differently, and each represented a unique color change. The color changes of Nin alone were interesting since it represented intensive and distinct color changes for some selected amines (TEA, TBA, DMCHA, and BZDMA).

The color difference maps (CDM) of the TMCDs towards different amines are represented in Fig. 3b. CDM can be used to explain which sensor does interact with which analyte. Using ImageJ software, the red, green, and blue (RGB) color intensity values of the total pixels of every spot in the array were extracted, and the average RGB values over all pixels of each spot related to PADs in Fig. 3a (after-exposure image) were calculated. The image of blank PADs (before-exposure image) was treated similarly. The following differences were found between the average color values of the sensors before and after exposure to the amines' odor:
∆ R =
R
̅
after exposure
−
R
̅
before exposure
∆ G =
G
̅
after exposure
−
G
̅
before exposure
∆ B =
B
̅
after exposure
−
B
̅
before exposure

To produce a CDM, ΔR, ΔG, and ΔB values as the analytical signals were used. As a result, three numbers corresponding to differences in the average values of R, G, and B were presented for each detection zone. So, a 27-length response data vector (9 sensing elements ×3 color elements) was gathered for each sample and recorded as a spreadsheet. Positive and negative values were both included in this vector. However, because the RGB color values are positive integers in the range of 0–255, the absolute values of the differences were taken into consideration when translating these differences to CDM. It should be noted in data analysis, the signs in the difference values were used without any changes. The response vector for each amine sample was obtained by averaging three repeated measurements. Remarkably, each amine possesses a unique colorimetric signature, allowing it to be identified. Additionally, it has been observed that the amines could be clustered based on the type of amine they belong to (primary, secondary, and tertiary amines). Specifically, amines belonging to the same category represent similar CDMs, offering valuable guidance to analysts in appropriately classifying amines according to their specific amine types.

The average color intensities of every sensing element (reported as the Euclidean distance of the RGB values) for all studied amines are shown in Fig. S7. This plot clearly shows the selectivity of the set of TMCD’ responses. There are not a pair of amines with the same response pattern. According to the results, among all sensor units, Ninhydrin showed a higher response for almost all amines.

The response signal outputs of the PAD towards all amines generated a training data matrix, which was first subjected to PCA and HCA as unsupervised clustering methods and then LDA as a supervised method. The results are given in Fig. 4. Spectacularly, all studied amines are clustered into distinct classes in the PCA and HCA models. The classification of the amines by LDA resulted in 100 % classification accuracy. It is observed from the PCA score plot that the amines of the same amine type (primary, secondary, and tertiary) are located close to each other. Therefore, a new LDA model was built, aiming to discriminate the amines based on their type. To do so, a 4-class LDA model was run. The results, which are shown in Fig. 5, clearly demonstrate the ability of the device to accurately classify the amines based on their type.

In Fig. 6, the discrimination of the amines of the same amine type is evident. As observed, all the studied amines were successfully discriminated in the factor space of the first three principal components. To find how many PCA dimensions are required for accurate classification of the amines, the PCA scores were fed successively into LDA models, and the modeling accuracies were calculated. As shown in Fig. S8, for accurate classification with 100 % accuracy, 4 principal components were required for all types of amines.

It is worth noting that the colorimetric array developed in this work was also able to identify isomers of some amines, for instance, nBA and sBA. Moreover, primary polyamines: ethylene diamine and triethylenetetramine were studied, and because of their different basic strengths, they appeared in separate clusters.

Alkyl groups donate electrons to the more electronegative nitrogen atom in alkylamines. The inductive effect makes the electron density of the alkylamine's nitrogen greater than the nitrogen of ammonia. The small amount of extra negative charge built up on the nitrogen atom makes the lone pair even more attractive to metal ions doped in carbon dots. In our studies, we examined a series of secondary amines like dimethyl amine, diethyl amine, diethanolamine, diisopropylamine, and dicyclohexylamine, and as one can see in Fig. 6, they appeared in a separate cluster. Moreover, tertiary alkyl amines like trimethylamine, tributylamine, tetramethylethylene diamine, n,n-dimethylcyclohexylamine, n-benzyldimethylamine, and a tertiary aryl amine p-dimethylamino benzaldehyde, were examined. It should be mentioned that the basicity of these amines not only depends on the inductive and resonance effects of groups but also on the group’s steric hindrance, which leads to a clear discrimination of different amines. To show the extended application of metal-doped carbon dots for amines detection, a series of cyclic amines like pyrolidine, pipyridine, pyridine, and 3,4-dimethyl pyridine were tested. Pyrolidine and pipyridine are more basic because of the inductive electron donative effects of alkyl groups in these secondary amines. In pyridine and 3,4-dimethyl pyridine, the nitrogen is sp2 hybridized, and increasing s-character brings electron pairs closer to the nitrogen nucleus, reducing their basicity and in the same time their tendency to bond to a metal cation, even though 3,4-dimethyl pyridine is more basic than pyridine because of the inductive electron-donating effects of methyl groups. To sum up, by using these metal-doped carbon dots, ammonia, primary, secondary, tertiary alkyl and aryl amines, as well as cyclic amines, were successfully discriminated based on their basicity (Fig. S9).

An important feature of the sensor arrays is their ability to analyze complex samples. So, besides successful discrimination of individual amines, our sensor array PAD was used to analyze the amines’ mixtures too. To do so, ternary and binary mixtures of three amines, including nBA, DMA, and TEA, were selected. The color change patterns of the sensor array for these mixtures and their PCA and HCA are shown in Fig. 7. Obviously, the individual amines and their ternary and binary mixtures have been discriminated into distinct clusters without any overlap in the factor space of the first 3 PCs. The same was observed from the HCA dendrograms.

Besides the ability of our sensor array for qualitative analysis, as was confirmed by the classification analysis, it was assessed for quantitative analysis too. In order to illustrate the reliability of the developed sensor array for quantitative determination, the Euclidean norm of the sensor array response was calculated and used as an analytical signal for deriving calibration curves. In Fig. 8, the obtained calibration curves for ethylamine (EA) and isopropylamine (iPA) are shown. Obviously, there is a logarithmic relationship between the Euclidean distance of the PAD response and the analyte’s concentration in the range of 0.008–1600 ppm (R2 =0.99), with a detection limit of 1.4 ppb for EA, and 0.05–828 ppm (R2 =0.99), with detection limits of 39 ppb for iPA. The color change patterns of the sensor array for a set of different concentrations of these two amines are also shown in Fig. 8. These impressive detection limits of these amines are generally well below the immediately dangerous to life or health (IDLH) limit for these amines (600 and 1400 mg L−1 for EA and iPA, respectively).

To measure the reproducibility of our PAD, 4 amines were selected, and each was measured 10 times by the PAD. It should be noted that each measurement was done by a new PAD, and hence the calculated standard deviations of 10 repeated measurements include between sensor variances too. As shown in Fig. S10, the relative standard deviations for 10 replicate measurements are less than 5 %.

In order to understand which sensing unit contributed more significantly to the amines’ discrimination, the factor loadings for the first three PCs of the PCA model were investigated. As shown in Fig. S11, all used indicators represented significant contributions to discrimination. However, S7 (Zn(II)-doped CDs) and S9 (no-doped CDs) for primary, S1 (Cr(III)-doped CDs), S4 (Co(II)-doped CDs), and S7 (Zn(II)-doped CDs) for secondary, and S1 (Cr(III)-doped CDs), S8 (Nin alone), and S9 (no-doped CDs) for tertiary amines showed more impact than the others.

Meat quality has always been an especially critical issue for the meat industry in the 21st century. One of the primary factors determining meat freshness during storage is the concentration of amines, the metabolites from the microbial decarboxylation of amino acids [35]. Numerous analytical techniques for monitoring meat spoilage have been developed, including HPLC [36], FT-IR spectrometry [37], fluorescence [38–40], and GC-MS [41,42]. Most of those methods, however, lack portability, require sophisticated instrumentation, or require time-consuming sample preparation. There remains, therefore, an urgent need for new methods for simple, rapid, and sensitive sensing of amine volatiles for application to the assessment of food and especially meat freshness.

In this study, three kinds of meat (fish, lamb, and chicken) were chosen and stored at room temperature (24 ± 1 °C) or in the kitchen refrigerator (4 ± 1 °C) for a length of time varying from 0 to 11 days. The responses of the sensor array to the headspace of the meat samples were collected over time. The obtained results from PCA model ( Figs. 9 and S12) demonstrated fine discrimination between meats stored at different times. It is worthy to mention that the color of the PAD for samples stored at 4 ± 1 °C compared to samples stored at 24 ± 1 °C showed less increase in intensity over the days. It was found that by combining the PAD with chemometric data analysis, the meat samples stored at 4 ± 1 °C and 24 ± 1 °C were mutually recognized at an early stage of spoilage with a low level of amines concentration. Obviously, the amines emitted from the meat stored at room temperature were of higher concentration than the meat stored in the refrigerator, so they were clearly discriminated in early times. PAD also showed more and faster spoilage for fish meat than the other two types of meat, and the progress of this spoilage could be traced through the amines emitted during the storage period.

As shown in Fig. S13, the sensor response represented linear relationships with the storage time, explaining the potential of our device to estimate the storage time. To evaluate the model performance, two new samples from fish and lamb were analyzed by the sensor and was sampled at different time intervals. As shown in the left part of Fig. S13, the test samples were located correctly to the storage time as the training samples. Moreover, the storage time of these test samples was predicted by the regression models of Fig. S1 and prediction errors less than 15 % were obtained.

In conclusion, a promising, portable and disposable colorimetric sensor array has been illustrated for the rapid qualitative identification of amines (and also some of their isomers). The sensing mechanism was based on ninhydrin-mediated differential interaction of some metal-doped carbon dots with different amines. Interestingly, ninhydrin represented different color changes when interacted with the carbon dots doped with different metal cations. Amines based on basicity and donating ability could interfere the ninhydrin-carbon dots interactions thus an array of colorimetric changes, which was unique for each studied amine, was observed. Moreover, the developed optical sensor array also represented ultra-sensitivity for quantitative analysis of individual amines in the vapor phase with a limit of detection in ppb ranges. This work successfully achieved the recognition and classification of different spoilage states in different kinds of meat. A comparison between our method and other reported sensor arrays for amine discrimination ( Table 1) explains that we could discriminated much larger number of amines using lower number of sensing reagents. In addition, the sensitivity of our device is much higher than almost of reported device. Our method reported the same detection limit compared to the method of Zhong et al. However, we discriminated a larger number of amines.

Motahereh Alimohammadi: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hoda Sharifi: Validation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Javad Tashkhourian: Conceptualization, Co-supervision, Methodology. Mohammad Vazan: Methodology. Mojtaba Shamsipur: Conceptualization, Validation. Bahram Hemmateenejad: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thankfully acknowledge financial support from Shiraz University Research Council.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134274.

ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Motahareh Alimohammadi received her B.S. degree in pure chemistry from Shiraz University in 2004. Then got her M.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Persian Gulf University in 2009. She start her Ph.D. program in Shiraz University in 2017. Her research interests focused on synthesis new nano materials and their applications on detection and determination of drug cancers and some biological compounds. During her Ph.D. program she received a scholarship to visit Texas A&M University and work on some new chiral solvating agents.

Hoda Sharifi received her B.S. and M.S. degrees in pure chemistry and analytical chemistry from Shahid Chamran University in 2012 and Ph.D. from Shiraz University in 2022. Her research interests focus on the development of paper-based analytical device for monitoring environmental hazardous materials and medical diagnosis. She has spent a ten-months period as a visiting researcher at Heidelberg University on paper-based analytical sensors.

Javad Tashkhourian received his M.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees in analytical chemistry from Shiraz University in 1999 and 2004, respectively. He was a member of chemistry department of Persian Gulf University (2004–2010). He joined the chemistry department of Shiraz University in 2010 where he is now an Associate Professor. His research is focused on design and construction of chemical sensors (optical and electrochemical) and synthesis and applications of nanomaterials in electrochemical analysis.

Mohammad Vazan received his B.S. and M.S. degrees in pure chemistry and analytical chemistry from Payame Noor University and Shiraz University in 2014 and 2022, respectively. His research interest is focused on the design and development of electrochemical sensors for the analysis of drugs and pharmaceuticals.

Mojtaba Shamsipur has a Ph.D. in analytical chemistry obtained at Michigan State University in 1979. He is currently a professor of chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran. His research work has been mainly focused on the thermodynamic and kinetic studies of macrocyclic ligand complexes, and their analytical applications in areas such as ion-transport through liquid membranes, solid-phase extraction, ion-selective electrodes, and optical sensors. From the last decade, his research area is also extended to fluorescent nanoparticles and their analytical applications. He has published over 1000 scientific papers in international journals.

Bahram Hemmateenejad is a professor of Chemistry at Chemistry Department of Shiraz University. He received his B.S. degree in pure chemistry from Shiraz University in 1996, M.S. and Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Isfahan University of Technology and Shiraz University in 1998 and 2002, respectively. As fellow of Alexander von Humboldt Foundation, he has visited different research institutes and universities in Germany. He won many national and international awards such as Razi Festival award, Analytical Chemistry Award of the Iranian Chemical Society, Elsevier Chemolab Award and TWAS young affiliate membership. He runs a Lab on the development and applications of different chemometrics methods in the chemical disciplines of bioanalytical chemistry, chemo-biological interactions, computer-aided drug design and colorimetric sensors. Currently he is very active on development of paper-based analytical sensors. He is associate editor of Journal of the Iran Chemical Society and author of more than 250 research papers and reviews. With an h-index of 44, his name has been appeared among top 2% scientists for 3 consecutive years.
